notes: Updated manually Mar 13 evening ET. Sources: CNBC, NPR, CNN, CBS, Al Jazeera, Wikipedia, Britannica, ACLED, Critical Threats, Alma Center, Foreign Policy, TRT World, IEA.

Daily launch series: Feb 28–Mar 9 based on best available public day-by-day reconstruction; Mar 10–11 anchored by official UAE daily counts; Mar 12–13 estimated from credible fragment range. No clean all-theater daily ledger exists for any date — these are working estimates.
Oil prices are market close or latest intraday as noted. Brent $99.32 and WTI $93.75 are Mar 13 AM ET (CNBC).
Iran casualty figures from Iran Health Ministry via Al Jazeera tracker. US deaths from CENTCOM/CNN. Displacement from UNHCR.
Attack wave counts (Israel-focused) from Alma Research Center. CENTCOM target count is official but independently unverified.
Business sections reflect analysis informed by Foreign Policy, TRT World, Chatham House, and OFAC regulatory structure — not legal advice.

metrics.desc: Since Feb 28 (Operation Epic Fury / True Promise IV)

metrics.label: Attack tempo ∆
metrics.desc: CENTCOM: ballistic down 90%, drone down 83% vs D1 (as of D6)

sitrep: Day 14 — Al-Quds Day under fire: Israel launched an 'extensive wave' of strikes on Tehran during mass Al-Quds Day rallies. An explosion hit near the march area. Top officials including security chief Larijani made defiant public appearances.
KC-135 crash: US refueling tanker crashed in western Iraq — all 6 crew killed. CENTCOM says not hostile fire; Iranian proxy claimed credit. Total US deaths now 13.

Escalation signals: Trump told Fox he'll 'hit Iran very hard over the next week.' Hegseth declared this would be 'highest volume of strikes yet' and said Mojtaba Khamenei is 'likely disfigured.' Hegseth also said US Navy 'not ready' to escort tankers through Hormuz yet.

Oil / economic: Brent holding ~$99 after closing above $100 yesterday (first since Aug 2022). US eased Russian oil sanctions to stabilize markets. Germany's Merz calls for 'convincing plan' to end war.

Diplomacy: UAE diplomat Nusseibeh: mediation 'can only happen when the guns go silent.' Pezeshkian's 3 conditions from yesterday (rights, reparations, guarantees) remain on the table. Iran internet blackout enters 3rd week.

Key trajectory signal: Iranian kinetic capability down 83–90% (CENTCOM D6 data), but conflict is widening not narrowing — Hezbollah fully active, shipping attacks escalating, IRGC threatening Gulf financial institutions. Shift from kinetic to economic warfare.

theoryEvaluation.title: Support for the theory
theoryEvaluation.body: Launch tempo has collapsed from 480+720 (D1) to single/low-double-digit missiles and sub-40 drones (D10–14). CENTCOM claims air dominance achieved by D6. IDF destroyed 300+ launchers. 90+ Iranian naval vessels destroyed. This is the steepest capability degradation curve in any modern conflict.

theoryEvaluation.title: What weakens confidence
theoryEvaluation.body: Iran has shifted from kinetic mass-launch to economic warfare: Hormuz closure, ship attacks, threatening Gulf financial institutions, proxy activation (Hezbollah). The 'capability' being suppressed is narrower than the threat — regime has adapted to use remaining assets asymmetrically. Oil at $100, Dow -700, gas up 15% all impose real costs. Also: Mar 10–13 daily counts are estimates, not clean official ledgers.

theoryEvaluation.title: Current judgment
theoryEvaluation.body: Launch capability is materially degraded — that part of the theory is holding. But the cost side is rising faster than expected. The regime is not behaving like a force that's been broken; it's behaving like a force that's shifted to a strategy designed to outlast coalition political willingness. The race between attrition and cost pressure is now roughly even.

scenarioProbabilities.name: US 'Mission Accomplished' + messy wind-down
scenarioProbabilities.body: Trump declares objectives met. Strikes taper. Hormuz gradually reopens under international escort. Oil settles $80–90. Most likely if domestic economic pressure (oil at $100, midterms) intensifies. Trump's mixed signals ('war over soon' vs. 'hit them hard') reflect this tension.

scenarioProbabilities.name: Protracted low-intensity conflict (months)
scenarioProbabilities.body: Strike tempo declines but never fully stops. Hormuz partially blockaded. Tit-for-tat drone/missile exchanges and proxy ops continue. Oil $90–110 sustained. No ceasefire, no resolution. 'Frozen hot conflict.' Ghalibaf: 'We aren't seeking a ceasefire.'

scenarioProbabilities.name: Negotiated ceasefire via mediation
scenarioProbabilities.body: China/Turkey/Oman framework. Pezeshkian demands: recognition of rights, reparations, no-future-attack guarantees. Challenging but possible if both sides seek off-ramp. Earliest window: late March. UAE: mediation 'only when guns go silent.'

scenarioProbabilities.name: Full regional war escalation
scenarioProbabilities.body: Major casualty event triggers ground/naval escalation. Hezbollah fully activated. Hormuz mined extensively (Iran has 2,000–6,000 mines per Soufan Center). Oil past $130. NATO drawn in via Cyprus/Turkey. Low probability but catastrophic.

scenarioProbabilities.name: Iranian regime fracture / internal crisis
scenarioProbabilities.body: Military losses + Khamenei succession crisis fracture IRGC. Mojtaba Khamenei's authority contested — Hegseth says 'likely disfigured.' Trump doubts civilian uprising ('big hurdle to climb for people without weapons'). Conflict ends via internal collapse rather than diplomacy.

scenarioProbabilities.name: Nuclear threshold crisis
scenarioProbabilities.body: Iran signals breakout as existential deterrent. IAEA found hidden enriched uranium pre-war. Parchin complex damage confirmed by satellite (ISIS). Would trigger international crisis management override and reshape all other scenarios.

dailySeries.sourceLabel: Feb 28–Mar 9: best available public reconstruction. Mar 10–11: anchored by official UAE daily counts. Mar 12–13: fragment-based estimates. No clean all-theater daily ledger exists.

oil.note: CNBC/LSEG market close or intraday. Pre-war baseline ~$65 Brent. Mar 7 = intraday peak (WTI hit $119 overnight before crash). Mar 12 = first Brent close above $100 since Aug 2022. Mar 13 = AM ET price.

latestTargetBreakdown.note: Latest daily slice estimate. UAE is most targeted country by volume (NPR/Horizon Insights). 206 total attack waves on Israel through D13 (Alma Center). Zeros = no public hit recorded, not guaranteed inactivity.

dailyRows.capability: Opening surge shows full usable inventory. Khamenei assassinated. IRGC closes Hormuz.
dailyRows.cost: Political shock. Oil jumps to $72 Brent. Iran retaliates within hours (Op True Promise IV).

dailyRows.capability: Peak intensity — launch architecture still largely intact. Beit Shemesh strike kills 9 in Israel.
dailyRows.cost: Dubai Airport struck. Oil $79. Regional anxiety expands. Khamenei death confirmed.

dailyRows.capability: Still high but no longer climbing. First hints of attrition or recalibration.
dailyRows.cost: Hezbollah enters war — fires into Israel for first time since Nov 2024 ceasefire. 20 killed Tehran Niloofar Square. Kurdish PJAK reportedly enters Iran.
dailyRows.assessment: Still high but off peak. Hezbollah second front opens.

dailyRows.capability: Sustained but sliding. IRIB HQ and parliament hit. US sub sinks IRIS Dena off Sri Lanka.
dailyRows.cost: Trump: Iran has 'no navy, air force, or radar.' Assembly of Experts bombed before supreme leader vote.
dailyRows.assessment: Sustained but sliding. Major infrastructure hits.

dailyRows.capability: Meaningful degradation visible. IDF: 300+ launchers destroyed. UAE: 174 missiles, 689 drones tracked since D1.
dailyRows.cost: 11-year-old killed by shrapnel in Kuwait. Insurance and shipping costs surging.

dailyRows.capability: CENTCOM: air dominance achieved. Ballistic attacks down 90%, drones down 83%. Fars: 500+ missiles, ~2,000 drones fired cumulative.
dailyRows.cost: Azadi Sport Complex bombed. Iranian drones hit Azerbaijan's Nakhchivan airport.
dailyRows.assessment: Capacity degradation clearer. CENTCOM claims air dominance.

dailyRows.capability: Selective rather than mass launch. 3,000+ targets struck, 43 warships destroyed.
dailyRows.cost: US war costs: $11.3B in first 6 days. Houthi leader: 'Our hands are on the trigger.'
dailyRows.assessment: Lower operational tempo. War cost alarm.

dailyRows.cost: Trump demands 'unconditional surrender.' Oil spikes to $120 intraday on Hormuz fears. Iran deploys mines. Iraq output collapses 70%.

dailyRows.capability: Residual launch pressure survives despite heavy attrition.
dailyRows.cost: WTI hits $119 overnight then crashes to $85 on Trump 'war ending' signals. G7 emergency oil release. Gulf storage full. Falak-ol-Aflak heritage site struck.

dailyRows.capability: Near-floor daily output. Mojtaba Khamenei appointed new Supreme Leader.
dailyRows.cost: Isfahan heritage sites damaged: Naqsh-e Jahan Square, Shah Mosque, Chehel Sotoun. Israeli strikes on Tehran oil facilities.

dailyRows.primary: UAE official count provides clearest daily slice
dailyRows.capability: Tempo remained materially below opening phase. CENTCOM: 5,000+ targets at ~1,000/day.
dailyRows.cost: 6 US soldiers killed in Kuwait strike. Hegseth vows 'most intense day.' Ghalibaf: 'We aren't seeking a ceasefire.' 1,200+ killed Iran, 570 Lebanon, 12 Israel.
dailyRows.assessment: Official UAE count anchor. US casualties mount.

dailyRows.capability: Launch activity remained lower-volume and selective.
dailyRows.cost: IEA releases record 400M barrels. 16 Iranian minelayers sunk. Ships attacked in Hormuz. Iran threatens to target Gulf banks and financial institutions. Dubai Airport hit by drones again. UNESCO urges heritage site protection.
dailyRows.assessment: Official UAE count. Economic warfare escalation.

dailyRows.primary: Fragmentary Gulf reporting; no clean all-theater ledger
dailyRows.capability: Constrained throughput, not a fresh mass-strike wave.
dailyRows.cost: Mojtaba Khamenei's first address: vows Hormuz stays closed. 3.2M displaced (UNHCR). Brent closes $100.46. Pezeshkian sets 3 ceasefire conditions. 90+ Iranian vessels destroyed. Dow -700.
dailyRows.assessment: Fragment-based estimate. New Supreme Leader speaks.

dailyRows.primary: UAE reporting + regional fragments. Al-Quds Day.
dailyRows.capability: Capability degraded vs opening phase but not eliminated. IRGC-Hezbollah joint ops claimed.
dailyRows.cost: Heavy Israeli strikes on Tehran during Al-Quds Day rallies. KC-135 crash kills 6 (13 total US deaths). Trump: 'hit very hard next week.' Hegseth: Khamenei 'likely disfigured.' US eases Russian oil sanctions. Brent $99.
dailyRows.assessment: Best-available estimate. Conflict widening not narrowing.

timeline.title: Immediate (next 72h)
timeline.body: Watch: IMO emergency session on Hormuz scheduled Mar 18–19. Whether Trump's 'hit them very hard next week' translates to sustained escalation or rhetorical peak. Oil reaction to Russian sanctions easing.

timeline.title: Near-term (next 4–10 days)
timeline.body: Fed interest rate decision next week amid $100 oil and inflation pressure. Energy Secretary Wright said Hormuz escort 'not ready' but possible by month end. If launch tempo stays in low-double-digit missile / sub-40 drone range, attrition case strengthens.

timeline.title: Decision horizon (next 1–3 weeks)
timeline.body: War direction turns on whether coalition decision-makers view launch suppression as sufficient before cost pressure becomes politically intolerable. Pezeshkian's ceasefire conditions provide a diplomatic framework — the question is whether anyone picks it up.

timeline.title: Medium-term (Apr–Jun)
timeline.body: FIFA World Cup 2026 in US this summer — Iran team status uncertain (sports minister says participation 'not possible'). Prolonged conflict shifts to protracted low-intensity scenario. If Hormuz stays closed 2+ months, Brent could test $135 (Rystad).

timeline.title: Main uncertainty
timeline.body: A mass-casualty strike on either side, wider proxy activation (Houthis have been restrained so far), or persistent Hormuz disruption could yank the timeline off its current path. The regime is not behaving like a broken force — it's adapted to economic warfare.

endstate.title: Most likely
endstate.body: A pressured de-escalation after meaningful attrition of launch capability, with both sides claiming success. Trump declares 'mission accomplished,' Iran claims survival. Hormuz reopens gradually. Timeline: 4–8 weeks from now.

endstate.title: Theory-aligned scenario
endstate.body: If missile and drone capability is broken before cost pressure forces a stop, coercive pressure on regime leadership rises sharply — but the regime has already adapted to economic warfare, reducing the leverage of pure kinetic suppression.

endstate.title: What would change this
endstate.body: Restored launch volume, Houthi activation, a Hormuz mine incident sinking a major tanker, or US domestic political backlash from oil/economic pain forcing an early operational pause. Also: evidence that Mojtaba Khamenei is incapacitated could create regime paralysis that accelerates or derails everything.

impactIranfarhang: Air freight: CRITICAL. Iranian airspace is active combat zone with 1,000+ daily US/Israeli strikes. No commercial cargo flights operating in/out of Iran. Atlanta reshipping node is intact but has nothing to reship. This is a complete new-inventory freeze.

Tehran publisher access: HIGH. Iran internet blackout (2+ weeks, per NetBlocks) cuts communication with publishers and suppliers. No reliable way to coordinate procurement or confirm inventory availability.

OFAC informational materials exemption: STABLE (LOW risk). The exemption (31 CFR §560.210(c)) is statutory — anchored in the Berman Amendment and IEEPA. It survived every prior escalation and is not at risk from this conflict. This is your legal bedrock.

Payment via France/KEMCO: MEDIUM-HIGH. French banking channels not directly disrupted, but EU banks are heightening Iran-related transaction scrutiny. Compliance departments may apply extra caution regardless of exemption status.
UPS domestic reshipping: LOW. Atlanta → US/Canadian institutions works normally. The domestic leg is unaffected.
University demand: POTENTIALLY INCREASING. Conflict drives academic and policy interest in Persian studies. Existing inventory may see accelerated demand.
prepIranfarhang: Maximize shipment of existing Atlanta inventory to fill current orders — this is your only near-term supply.
Proactive client communications: frame as temporary wartime logistics disruption, not compliance issue. The exemption is intact.
Explore whether any inventory can be sourced from Iranian diaspora publishers outside Iran (Turkey, possibly Europe).
Protect periodicals and subscription continuity first — these have the highest reputational cost if disrupted.
Keep duplicate metadata, invoices, and packing records outside Iran to preserve service continuity.
Monitor for air freight corridor reopening — this will be the first signal of business resumption.
Do NOT change payment structures reactively — the KEMCO/France channel is the most defensible setup available.
signalsIranfarhang: Iranian outbound air-freight resumption (even partial) — first sign of supply chain unfreezing.
Iran internet restoration (even partial) — enables publisher communication.
Repeated customs holds or manual inspections affecting informational-material shipments at US entry.
University or library procurement teams asking for heightened sanctions review or pausing approvals.
EU banking actions that specifically impact France-Iran payment corridors.
Any new OFAC guidance or executive orders mentioning informational materials (extremely unlikely but worth monitoring).
impactKIP: Iran-UAE corridor: NEAR-TOTAL DISRUPTION. Hormuz closed (vessel traffic 20% of normal). Jebel Ali expects reduced calls. Dubai Airport hit by drones. AWS data center struck. 70%+ Gulf flights canceled. UAE/Kuwait exchanges suspended. Full logistics chain broken at multiple points simultaneously.

UAE banking: HIGH and RISING. This is the biggest change from pre-war. TRT World reports UAE actively considering freezing Iran-linked assets and targeting shadow companies/currency exchanges. Chatham House analyst: 'Economic engagement strategy has failed.' Iran struck UAE — 6 killed, 131 wounded. Citigroup told staff to work from home. PwC closed Gulf offices.
Exchange house settlement: HIGH. Iran's threat to target Gulf financial institutions (announced Mar 11) further increases de-risking pressure. Exchange houses face heightened regulatory scrutiny.

Direct asset seizure: MEDIUM (upgraded from LOW). UAE withdrew ambassador from Tehran. Gulf states pushing UNSC resolution condemning Iran. Gargash called Iran 'primary source of danger.' Analysts note UAE asset freeze could mirror Western actions against Russia. Iranian attacks on Emirati soil make the 'pressure release valve' approach much harder to maintain.

Shipping / re-export: CRITICAL. Major shipping companies have issued 'End of Voyage' notices for Gulf routes. Cargo may be discharged at alternate ports. New surcharges apply. Jebel Ali throughput declining.
prepKIP: URGENT: Assess exposure of any funds held in UAE bank accounts — consider moving liquid assets to less exposed jurisdictions if possible before potential freeze actions.
Document all legitimate trade records thoroughly — if UAE imposes asset reviews, having clean documentation is critical for distinguishing legitimate trade from grey-zone networks.
Identify alternative logistics corridors (Turkey is most viable; Oman also hit but less disrupted).
Map each payment flow to its bank, exchange house, and beneficial-owner exposure. Know exactly which channels are at risk.
Shorten receivable cycles and model working-capital stress under 7, 14, and 30-day disruption cases.
Pre-write counterparty documentation packs for fast compliance responses.
Monitor UAE regulatory announcements daily — the shift from 'tolerate grey-zone trade' to 'crack down on Iranian networks' could happen rapidly.
Have contingency plans for complete loss of UAE banking access — this is no longer a tail risk.
signalsKIP: UAE bank account closures or widened transaction rejections for Iran-linked clients — the most important early warning.
Exchange-house restrictions, monitoring crackdowns, or licensing actions in Dubai.
Official UAE announcements regarding Iranian-linked asset freezes or shadow company targeting.
Jebel Ali or related Gulf cargo holds, reroutes, or documentation surges.
Shipping notices shifting liability or forcing end-of-voyage changes mid-route.
UNSC resolution passage specifically condemning Iran's Gulf strikes (would give UAE political cover for financial crackdown).
Any resumption of Hormuz transit (even escorted) — would be the first logistics de-escalation signal.
